summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques a New York Times editorial arguing that America needs to do more for 'gifted' students (defined as high test-scorers). She challenges both the premise that this is a crisis and the proposed solutions, arguing that bright kids who disengage from school can find valuable learning through work experiences, self-directed reading, and real-world adventures. She points out that test-taking ability correlates with wealth and practice rather than innate talent, meaning any program focused on high scorers will primarily benefit already-privileged kids. The piece concludes that doubling down on elite academic tracking would make elite youth more elite without benefiting the country.
key_points: America's supposed crisis of underperforming gifted students is based on standardized test comparisons that may not reflect actual technological or scientific competitiveness
When bright kids 'check out' of school, the solution isn't necessarily more AP classes—it could be giving them meaningful real-world experiences like jobs and internships
Separating, isolating, and venerating great test-takers is the opposite of how to develop future successful people
Test-taking ability is not a pure measure of talent; it's heavily influenced by practice and wealth, so programs targeting high scorers will be dominated by 'the usual suspects'
Modern resources like online communities give nerdy kids more options for self-directed learning than ever before
money_quotes: If that's true, if bright kids who aren't academically challenged at school start checking out more and more, then it makes just as much sense to me to see if there's something they can check out towards.
I learned way more about how the world worked than I would have if I'd followed the advice of this editorial, which was to take lots more AP classes and then enter college when I was 14.
Enrichment happens when kids learn about stuff that's outside their usual rhythm and when there are no adults scripting their activities and telling them what to do or how many laps to swim.

What drives me nuts just as much as the idea of further separating and isolating and venerating great test-takers, which as far as I'm concerned is the opposite way you should treat future successful people, is the idea that there should be such a well-defined funnel for children at all.
The idea that, just because someone's good at tests they should be treated as if they're already running the Fed only increases the weird worshippy aspect of how our culture treats math nerds.
It's just plain untrue that test-taking actually exposes talent. It's well established that you can get better at these tests through practice, and that richer kids practice more.
This is a plan to make elite youth even more elite. And I don't know about you, but my feeling is that's not going to help our country overall.
key_insight: Programs designed to help 'gifted' students based on test scores don't identify talent—they amplify existing privilege while ignoring that real enrichment comes from self-directed experiences outside scripted academic funnels.
